content.problemStatement:
Chef is deriving weird ways to sort his array. Currently he is trying to sort his arrays in increasing order by reversing some of his subarrays.

To make it more challenging for himself, Chef decides that he can reverse only those subarrays that have sum of its elements **at most** XXX. Soon he notices that it might not be always possible to sort the array with this condition.

Can you help the Chef by telling him if the given array can be sorted by reversing subarrays with sum at most XXX.

More formally, for a given array AAA and an integer XXX, check whether the array can be sorted in increasing order by reversing some (possibly none) of the subarrays such that the sum of all elements of the subarray is **at most** XXX.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains of two space-separated integers NNN and XXX denoting the length of the array and the maximum sum of subarrays that you can reverse.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2,..., A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​ representing the initial array.


Output Format
For each test case, output YES\texttt{YES}YES if Chef can sort the array using a finite number of operations, else output NO\texttt{NO}NO.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings YeS\texttt{YeS}YeS, yEs\texttt{yEs}yEs, yes\texttt{yes}yes and YES\texttt{YES}YES will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X — the length of the array and the maximum allowed sum of a subarray that can be reversed. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the array can be sorted in non‑decreasing order using any number of reversals of subarrays whose sum is at most X, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: already sorted. Test case 2: element 2 > X and is out of place, so impossible. Test case 3: all misplaced elements are ≤ X, so sorting is possible.

hidden.label: edge case all large but sorted

solution.approach: Let B be the sorted version of the given array A. An element whose value is greater than X can never belong to a subarray whose sum ≤ X, therefore it cannot be moved by any allowed operation. Consequently, every element with value > X must already be at its final sorted position. For all indices i where A[i] ≠ B[i] we must be able to move both the current element A[i] and the element that should be placed there B[i]; this is possible only when both values are ≤ X. Hence the condition for a YES answer is: for every i, if A[i] ≠ B[i] then A[i] ≤ X and B[i] ≤ X. The algorithm sorts a copy of the array, then checks this condition in O(N) time. Overall complexity per test case is O(N log N) for sorting, which satisfies the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for the copy of the array

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it)); x = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
        b = sorted(a)
        ok = True
        for ai, bi in zip(a, b):
            if ai != bi and (ai > x or bi > x):
                ok = False
                break
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
